content.text: Feeding raw documents — PDFs, spreadsheets, slide decks, and scanned images — directly into a language model pipeline is one of the most high-value automation patterns available to agentic systems. Instead of requiring a human to copy-paste relevant passages, the agent ingests the document, extracts structured data according to a prompt, and drives downstream actions without manual intervention. This is the foundation of document-aware agents used in contract review, invoice automation, regulatory compliance, and enterprise knowledge extraction.

Document prompting is the practice of supplying a structured or semi-structured file — rather than free-form conversational text — as the primary input to a language model or multimodal model. The document can be a native PDF, a Word file, an Excel workbook, a presentation, a scanned image set, or any other formatted artifact that carries information beyond plain prose.
At the prompt level, the agent pairs the document (or excerpts of it) with a task instruction:
"Extract all line items with amounts and due dates"
"Identify clauses that deviate from the standard template"
. The model processes layout, tables, headers, and figures holistically, returning structured output the orchestrator can act on.

Because modern multimodal models can read page images directly, document prompting covers two technical paths: (1) vision-based, where each page is rasterised and sent as an image, and (2) text-based, where a parser extracts the plain text or Markdown representation and sends that. Production pipelines often combine both — using text for dense prose sections and vision for tables, charts, and form fields that lose fidelity when stripped to plain text.

Knowledge work produces documents, not database rows. An accounts-payable department receives hundreds of supplier invoices in PDF format every month. A legal team reviews contracts that span dozens of pages. A procurement officer compares RFP responses across multiple vendors. In each case, the information locked inside formatted files must be extracted before it can be reasoned over or acted on.

Before multimodal LLMs, this required either (a) brittle rule-based parsers that broke on every new layout, or (b) expensive manual data entry. Document prompting unlocks a third path: instruct a capable model to read the document and return exactly the fields or decisions you need, with provenance references back to the source page.

For agentic systems specifically, document prompting is the entry point for grounding: the agent cannot reason about a contract it has never read. Feeding the document into the context window (or into a retrieval step that feeds relevant chunks) gives the agent factual grounding that prevents hallucination of specific details like dollar amounts, dates, and named parties.
Agents can process an inbox of 500 invoices overnight with no human in the loop for routine cases, escalating only ambiguous ones.
Multimodal models read tables, headers, footers, and multi-column layouts without bespoke parsing rules per template.
Prompts constrain the response to a JSON schema, so downstream code receives typed fields rather than prose to parse.
Prompts can request page numbers or section references alongside extracted values, enabling auditors to verify each output against the source.
A single pipeline can process PDFs, scanned images, DOCX, XLSX, and HTML with the same prompt template, switching only the pre-processing step.
Replacing dozens of format-specific extraction rules with a single model prompt dramatically lowers maintenance overhead when vendors change invoice layouts.
A 200-page contract cannot fit in a single call. Chunking is mandatory, and merging results across chunks adds orchestration complexity.
Scanned documents with low image resolution, skew, or noise produce poor OCR text; garbage in, garbage out.
Processing each page as an image token is more expensive than sending plain text. A 50-page PDF at 1,000 tokens per page adds up quickly across a high-volume pipeline.
If a field is absent from the document but included in the extraction schema, some models will invent a plausible value. Schema validation and confidence scores mitigate this.
Multi-chunk sequential processing of a long document adds latency. Parallel chunk processing requires concurrency management in the orchestrator.
Two runs of the same document can return slightly different extracted values, making regression testing harder than with deterministic parsers.
The core pipeline has five stages: ingestion, pre-processing, chunking, model call, and result merging.
The raw file is received (from an email attachment, an S3 bucket, or an API upload). Metadata is logged: filename, MIME type, page count, file hash for deduplication.
The document is converted into a representation the model can consume. For native PDFs, two paths are available: (a) extract embedded text using a library such as
, or (b) rasterise each page to an image using
. For scanned PDFs with no embedded text, OCR (Tesseract, AWS Textract, Google Document AI) is applied first. Tables are often extracted separately using layout detection models.

If the document exceeds the model's context window, it is split into chunks. Chunking strategies include: fixed-page windows (send N pages per call), semantic splitting (split at section headings or paragraph boundaries), and relevance filtering (use a cheaper retrieval step to select only the pages likely to contain the target information).
Each chunk gets a metadata header embedded in the prompt: which document it came from, which pages it covers, and any preceding context summary. This preserves cross-chunk coherence.
Stage 4 — Model call with structured prompt

Each chunk is sent to the LLM with a prompt that specifies the extraction task and the desired output schema. For a multimodal model, page images are included as image blocks alongside the text prompt. The model returns a JSON object conforming to the schema — or a structured refusal if the required information is absent.

The orchestrator collects per-chunk results and merges them: deduplicating repeated mentions of the same field, resolving conflicts (e.g., a total that appears differently on page 1 and page 12), and computing a final confidence-weighted value. Fields with low confidence are flagged for human review.

The diagram below shows the full document prompting pipeline, from raw file sources through pre-processing and chunking to the multimodal LLM call, structured output, orchestrator merge, and downstream agent action.

attrs.alt: Document Prompting Pipeline — from document sources through chunking, multimodal LLM, and orchestrator to downstream agent action

content.text: Key architectural observations: (1) the chunker sits between pre-processing and the LLM, insulating the model call from raw file formats; (2) the orchestrator is the only component that sees all per-chunk outputs and is responsible for resolving conflicts; (3) low-confidence outputs loop back into the orchestrator for retry or escalation, not directly to the LLM, to avoid redundant API calls.
The information lives inside formatted files (PDFs, DOCX, XLSX) rather than a structured database or API.
The document layout is variable — different vendors, different templates — making rule-based parsers impractical to maintain.
The task requires understanding across multiple sections (e.g., cross-referencing a clause on page 3 with a definition on page 1).
You need traceability — extracted values should cite page numbers so humans can verify them.
The document contains visual elements (charts, diagrams, stamped forms) that plain-text extraction would lose.
Do NOT use document prompting when:
The data is already in a structured format (SQL database, clean CSV, REST API) — use a query or API call instead.
Strict determinism is required and any non-determinism is unacceptable (e.g., legal contract clause binding for automated signing) — use a human-in-the-loop review step before action.
The document is extremely long (1,000+ pages) and all pages need extraction in a single low-latency call — batch the work across time or use a specialised document extraction service.
The steps below walk through building a production-ready document prompting pipeline in Python using the Anthropic Claude API with native PDF support.
pip install anthropic pymupdf pillow pydantic

import fitz  # pymupdf
import base64
from pathlib import Path


def pdf_to_page_images(pdf_path: str, dpi: int = 150) -> list[dict]:
    """Convert each PDF page to a base64-encoded PNG for multimodal input."""
    doc = fitz.open(pdf_path)
    pages = []
    for page_num, page in enumerate(doc, start=1):
        mat = fitz.Matrix(dpi / 72, dpi / 72)
        pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=mat, alpha=False)
        img_bytes = pix.tobytes("png")
        img_b64 = base64.standard_b64encode(img_bytes).decode()
        pages.append({
            "page": page_num,
            "media_type": "image/png",
            "data": img_b64,
        })
    doc.close()
    return pages
Step 4 — Call the model with an extraction prompt

import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel


class InvoiceFields(BaseModel):
    vendor_name: str | None
    invoice_number: str | None
    invoice_date: str | None
    total_amount: float | None
    currency: str | None
    line_items: list[dict]
    confidence: float  # 0.0 – 1.0 self-reported


client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SYSTEM = """You are a precise document extraction assistant.
Return ONLY valid JSON matching the schema provided. If a field is absent,
return null. Set confidence to your estimated accuracy (0.0–1.0)."""

def extract_invoice_chunk(chunk: list[dict], schema: str) -> dict:
    """Send one chunk of page images and return parsed JSON."""
    image_blocks = [
        {
            "type": "image",
            "source": {
                "type": "base64",
                "media_type": p["media_type"],
                "data": p["data"],
            },
        }
        for p in chunk
    ]
    page_range = f"{chunk[0]['page']}–{chunk[-1]['page']}"
    text_block = {
        "type": "text",
        "text": (
            f"Pages {page_range} of the invoice document are shown above.\n"
            f"Extract the following schema as JSON:\n{schema}"
        ),
    }
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": image_blocks + [text_block]}],
    )
    import json
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

def merge_invoice_results(chunk_results: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """Combine per-chunk extractions, preferring higher-confidence values."""
    merged = {
        "vendor_name": None,
        "invoice_number": None,
        "invoice_date": None,
        "total_amount": None,
        "currency": None,
        "line_items": [],
        "confidence": 0.0,
    }
    best_conf = 0.0
    for result in chunk_results:
        conf = result.get("confidence", 0.0)
        for field in ("vendor_name", "invoice_number", "invoice_date",
                      "total_amount", "currency"):
            if result.get(field) is not None and conf >= best_conf:
                merged[field] = result[field]
        merged["line_items"].extend(result.get("line_items", []))
        if conf > best_conf:
            best_conf = conf
    merged["confidence"] = best_conf
    # Deduplicate line items by description
    seen = set()
    deduped = []
    for item in merged["line_items"]:
        key = item.get("description", "")
        if key not in seen:
            seen.add(key)
            deduped.append(item)
    merged["line_items"] = deduped
    return merged
This minimal example sends a single page image (or a short text extract) and asks for structured output. It is a good starting point for prototyping.
This example processes a long PDF contract, splits it into 5-page windows, extracts termination and liability clauses from each window in parallel using

A mid-sized logistics company processes 3,000 supplier invoices per month, received as PDF email attachments. Previously, a team of four clerks spent 60% of their time manually keying vendor names, invoice numbers, line items, and totals into the ERP system.

The agentic pipeline works as follows. An email webhook triggers a Lambda function when a new attachment arrives. The function downloads the PDF, pushes it to an S3 bucket, and enqueues a job. A worker process pulls the job, rasterises each page using PyMuPDF at 150 DPI, splits into 5-page windows, and fans out parallel Claude API calls. Each call returns a JSON object with vendor, invoice number, date, line items, and total amount, plus a self-reported confidence score. The orchestrator merges results and validates: does the sum of line item amounts equal the stated total (within 1 cent)? If yes and confidence is above 0.9, the record is auto-posted to the ERP via its REST API. Otherwise, it is placed in a human review queue.

Results after three months: 78% straight-through processing (no human touch), average processing time reduced from 4 minutes per invoice to 22 seconds, and error rate on auto-processed invoices of 0.4% — below the 1.2% baseline error rate for manual entry. The remaining 22% of invoices requiring human review are either scanned documents with poor image quality or invoices with line items spanning multiple pages where the merge step flags a total mismatch.
Sending the entire document in one call

Stuffing a 50-page PDF into a single API call either hits the context limit or incurs enormous cost. Even when it fits, the model's attention degrades for content buried in the middle of a very long context.
The fix: always chunk documents. For retrieval tasks, use an embedding-based pre-filter to select the 5–10 most relevant pages before calling the large model.
Omitting page references from the prompt
If the prompt does not ask the model to include page or section references in the extracted output, the result is unauditable. Auditors cannot verify where a figure came from.
The fix: include in the prompt
"Include the page number where each value was found"

Models sometimes hallucinate a numeric field, especially when the field is absent from the document but present in the schema. Downstream code that uses the number directly can write incorrect values to a database.

The fix: after extraction, compute the checksum in code (e.g., sum of line items equals header total), and reject or flag any result where the check fails. Never rely on the model to catch its own arithmetic errors.
Using the same chunk boundary for every document type

A fixed 5-page window works for invoices where all fields are on page 1. For contracts where a clause definition on page 1 is referenced again on page 45, the per-chunk extraction will miss the connection.
The fix: adapt chunking to the document type. For long contracts, include a one-paragraph "context summary of preceding pages" in each chunk prompt so the model knows what came before.
Constrain the model to a Pydantic or JSON Schema definition rather than asking for free-form prose. This makes downstream parsing deterministic.
Ask the model to rate its own extraction confidence (0.0–1.0) for each field. Route low-confidence results to human review automatically.
Store the source page number alongside each extracted value for auditability and debugging.
Never trust math the model did inside the prompt. Recompute totals and checksums programmatically after extraction.
Use a small embedding model or keyword search to select relevant pages before sending them to the expensive multimodal model.
Handle the three document types separately.
Native digital PDF (extract text), high-quality scan (OCR then text), low-quality scan (vision-first). Each has different optimal preprocessing.
Hash the document and store the extraction result; skip re-processing if the hash already exists in the cache. This prevents double-billing on retries.
Collect at least 50 real documents of each type and build a regression suite. Document layouts change; automated tests catch regressions before production.
Document prompting throughput is constrained by three factors: API rate limits, per-page image token cost, and pre-processing time. Optimising each independently yields multiplicative improvement.

A rasterised page at 150 DPI typically encodes to ~800–1,200 image tokens. At 1,000 tokens per page × 50 pages × $15 per million input tokens, one document costs about $0.75 in input tokens. At volume, pre-filtering to only the relevant 10 pages drops this to $0.15 per document — a 5× cost reduction.
Pages are independent after chunking. Fanning out N chunk calls concurrently reduces wall-clock latency from O(N) to O(1), bounded by the API rate limit. Use

with a concurrency cap (typically 4–8 workers) to fan out all chunk calls in parallel while staying within vendor rate limits. The contract extraction example in the Examples section above demonstrates this pattern with

Rasterising and OCR-ing a document is CPU-intensive. Cache the page images (as base64 strings in Redis or S3) keyed by document hash + DPI. On retry or reprocessing, skip pre-processing entirely. For frequently accessed reference documents (e.g., a company's standard contract template), use Claude's prompt caching to avoid re-uploading the same image tokens on every call.

At scale (10,000+ documents per day), a queue-based architecture outperforms synchronous HTTP. Documents land in SQS, workers pull batches of 10, process in parallel, and write results to DynamoDB. Auto-scaling the worker fleet based on queue depth keeps latency predictable under load spikes.
Fast native PDF rendering and text extraction. Best for rasterising pages at configurable DPI.
Detailed layout-preserving text extraction, including bounding boxes for each character and word. Useful when text positions matter.
Lightweight pure-Python PDF reader; good for metadata and simple text extraction without rendering.
Wraps Poppler's pdftoppm. Produces high-fidelity rasters; requires Poppler installed.
Open-source OCR. Works well for clean scans; degrades on low resolution or handwriting.
Managed OCR with table and form detection. Strong accuracy on business documents; per-page pricing.
Specialised processors for invoices, W2s, receipts. Returns structured data rather than raw text.
Pre-built models for invoices, receipts, business cards, and ID documents.
Document loaders for PDF, DOCX, HTML; chunk splitters; retrieval chains. Good for rapid prototyping.
Purpose-built for document ingestion and indexing. Rich set of node parsers and metadata extractors.
Direct API access with support for the Files API (native PDF upload), prompt caching, and streaming.
Open-source library for parsing unstructured documents into structured elements; handles DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, PDF, images.
Very low — breaks on new layouts
Low — needs labelled training data per layout
Low inference cost, high training cost
Standard business docs: invoices, receipts, IDs
Variable layouts, complex instructions, cross-section reasoning
Q&A over a large document corpus — not point extraction

Documents fed to a third-party LLM API may contain sensitive PII, trade secrets, or legally protected information. Before sending, assess whether the vendor's data processing agreement covers the document class. For regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal), consider: (a) using an on-premise or VPC-deployed model, (b) redacting PII before the API call and re-inserting it into the structured output afterward, or (c) using the API vendor's zero-data-retention option.

A malicious document can contain hidden text (white text on white background, tiny font, steganographic encoding) that instructs the model to override the extraction task — a document-borne prompt injection attack. Mitigations include: (a) sanitise the text representation before insertion into the prompt, (b) use vision-only mode for untrusted documents so the raw text never enters the prompt, (c) validate that the output conforms strictly to the JSON schema and reject any output containing instruction-like language.

If the OCR step produces garbled text (rotated page, low contrast), the model will extract incorrect values with high confidence. Defend against this by adding an image quality check before the OCR step — compute the page's average contrast and skip OCR if it falls below a threshold, routing the document to a human queue instead.

API calls can fail transiently. Implement idempotency by storing the document hash + schema hash as a cache key. On retry, return the cached result rather than calling the API again. This prevents duplicate ERP entries when a Lambda retries after a timeout.

For regulated use cases, store: the input document hash, the exact prompt used, the model version, the raw model response, the parsed structured output, and the validation check results. This gives a complete audit trail if an extraction is disputed.

Document prompting is the foundational pattern for agents that process PDFs, spreadsheets, and other formatted files — it replaces brittle rule-based parsers with prompt-driven, layout-flexible extraction.
Always chunk long documents into page windows; never assume the whole document fits in one context window, and merge per-chunk results in the orchestrator.
Structured output schemas (JSON Schema, Pydantic) with self-reported confidence scores are essential: they make downstream parsing deterministic and enable automated quality gates.
Validate extracted numbers in code — recompute totals and checksums programmatically, never trust the model's arithmetic.
Include source page references in the extraction schema so every field is traceable to the source document for audit and debugging.
Pre-filter pages with cheap embedding retrieval before sending them to the expensive multimodal model; this cuts input token costs by 5–10× on long documents.
Prompt injection through malicious document content is a real attack surface — sanitise text representations and validate that all model output conforms strictly to the expected schema.
For regulated industries, evaluate on-premise model deployment, PII redaction before API calls, and zero-data-retention API options to satisfy data residency and privacy requirements.
A complete audit trail — document hash, prompt, model version, raw response, parsed output, validation result — is non-negotiable for financial and legal document automation.
